An editorial examination of why accountability must precede reconciliation, and how leadership systems often delay responsibility in the name of stability.

Accountability Is a Design Problem
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/accountability-is-a-design-problem
Post 8 of the Unaccountable series examines why goodwill and culture alone cannot sustain accountability, and why healthy leadership systems must design structural correction into authority itself.

Affinity Dressed as Service
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/affinity-dressed-as-service
The leader most likely to miss favoritism is not the tyrant. It is the relational leader — the one who genuinely invests in people and believes deeply that their job is to serve. This entry examines how affinity masquerades as servant leadership and what Greenleaf's original test demands of every leader who claims the title.

After the Smoke Clears
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/after-the-smoke-clears
A conclusion to the Narcissistic Leadership Tactics series, shifting focus from unhealthy patterns to the posture and practices of healthy, servant leadership.

Are You a Leader Who Puts Your Words Into Action?
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/words-into-action
Servant leadership is not measured in declarations. It is measured in days. A look at what congruence actually costs, why the leader who has never failed is the most dangerous kind, and what it means to put your words into action.

Build It On Purpose
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/build-it-on-purpose
Reflections from a talk given to first-generation college-bound students on purpose, discipline, curiosity, failure, and why the real legacy isn’t what you build, but who you help become better.

Clearing the Path
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/clearing-the-path
Servant leadership isn’t about getting people to make your job easier. It’s about making their path clear so they can do the work they were built to do.

DARVO: When the Guilty Become the Victim
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/darvo-when-the-guilty-become-the-victim
DARVO is a pattern used by offenders to deny behavior, attack the challenger, and reverse victim and offender roles. This entry examines how it works, why servant leaders are the specific target, the systems that make it sustainable, and what institutional courage actually requires.

How Leaders Lose the Capacity for Accountability
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/how-leaders-lose-the-capacity-for-accountability
A science-backed editorial examining how unchecked power reshapes perception, erodes accountability, and enables leaders to move from negligence to intentional harm without ever announcing the shift.

How Pressure Triggers Threat in Unaccountable Leaders
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/how-pressure-triggers-threat-in-unaccountable-leaders
An applied neuroscience examination of why ordinary accountability demands are processed as threat by unaccountable leaders, and how power, nervous system adaptation, and control shape leadership behavior.

How Unaccountable Leadership Becomes Structural
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/how-unaccountable-leadership-becomes-structural
An editorial examination of how unaccountable leadership endures, not through personality, but through systems that reorganize to protect authority from consequence.

I’ve spent some time purposely not writing.
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ive-spent-some-time-purposely-not-writing
A reflective post about stepping back from constant output to rediscover purpose in the process, challenging the 'arrival fallacy' and redefining success as becoming, not winning.

Leadership bottlenecks will choke the life out of a business.
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leadership-bottlenecks-will-choke-the-life-out-of-a-business
An exploration of how leadership bottlenecks form when fear, insecurity, and control stifle innovation, and how trust, curiosity, and flexibility restore growth.

Leadership Is Not a Clenched Fist, but a Guiding Hand (Part 1)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leadership-is-not-a-clenched-fist-but-a-guiding-hand-part-1
Part one introduces the metaphor of the clenched fist versus the guiding hand, contrasting control-based leadership with trust-based leadership rooted in strength, humility, and patience.

Leadership Is Not a Clenched Fist, but a Guiding Hand (Part 2)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leadership-is-not-a-clenched-fist-but-a-guiding-hand-part-2
Part two explores what real leadership looks like in action, listening before directing, protecting your people, teaching instead of telling, and leading with consistency through storms.

Leadership Is Not a Clenched Fist, but a Guiding Hand (Part 3)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leadership-is-not-a-clenched-fist-but-a-guiding-hand-part-3
Part three reveals the real cost and long-term return of leading with a guiding hand, time, patience, and sacrifice traded for trust, growth, and legacy that lasts.

Leadership isn’t a scoreboard.
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leadership-isnt-a-scoreboard
This post reminds leaders that their team's success is not competition but confirmation of great leadership, celebrating growth, shared wins, and lasting culture.

Leadership, Shoes, and Melted Feet
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leadership-shoes-and-melted-feet
A humorous but insightful reflection on empathy in leadership, reminding both owners and employees to walk in each other's shoes to prevent misunderstanding and burnout.

Leading Well Under Bad Leadership
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/leading-well-under-bad-leadership
The final entry in the Unaccountable Leadership series examining how leaders maintain integrity and influence when the system above them refuses accountability.

LONE WOLVES DIE YOUNG
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/lone-wolves-die-young
A challenge to the myth of the 'lone wolf' leader, reminding that isolation weakens leadership while community, collaboration, and shared purpose sustain success.

Mean People Suck (Especially When We Put Them in Charge)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/mean-people-suck
A first-person editorial reflection on how meanness is tolerated, and even rewarded, in leadership, and why authoritarian and narcissistic behavior corrodes organizations from the inside out.

Most people hear. Few actually listen.
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/most-people-hear-few-actually-listen
A reflection on the difference between hearing and listening in leadership, seeing beyond surface reactions to understand the real needs behind behavior.

Power vs. Authority: Part 1 — The Hunger
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/power-vs-authority-part-1-the-hunger
The pursuit of power and the earning of authority look identical from the outside at first. Both involve influence. Both involve people following a direction. The difference lives underneath, in motive, in method, and in what survives. Part 1 of this series examines the hunger that precedes power, where it comes from, what it costs the person chasing it, and what it leaves behind in the people who were close enough to watch.

Power vs. Authority: Part 2 — The Build
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/power-vs-authority-part-2-the-build
Most writing on earning authority makes it sound like a program. The build is not a program. It is a direction held under pressure, repeatedly, over enough time that the people around you stop waiting for the exception.

Power vs. Authority: Part 3 — The Test
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/power-vs-authority-part-3-the-test
What separates power from authority when something goes wrong. Crisis and pressure are the only real diagnostic. Power relies on the org chart when it needs people most. Authority calls and people show up.

Psychology of Servant Leadership, Part 1: The Servant Mindset
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/psychology-of-servant-leadership-part-1
Most people mistake servant leadership for softness. In reality, it’s power under control, strength aimed at serving others, not inflating ego.

Psychology of Servant Leadership, Part 2: Safety Before Strategy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/psychology-of-servant-leadership-part-2
Strategy doesn’t fail because it’s bad, it fails because people are too afraid to tell the truth, ask for help, or take intelligent risks.

Psychology of Servant Leadership, Part 3: Motivation and Meaning
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/psychology-of-servant-leadership-part-3
Servant leaders don’t squeeze results out of people, they design systems that satisfy the human needs that make motivation renewable.

Psychology of Servant Leadership, Part 4: Flourishing and Flow
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/psychology-of-servant-leadership-part-4
High performance isn’t about red-lining people, it’s about aligning pressure, clarity, and trust so teams can live in the flow state more often.

Psychology of Servant Leadership, Part 5: The Paradox of the Servant
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/psychology-of-servant-leadership-part-5
Servant leadership collapses when the leader abandons themselves. Boundaries aren’t the opposite of service, they’re what make it sustainable.

Psychology of Servant Leadership, Part 6: The System of Service
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/psychology-of-servant-leadership-part-6
Servant leadership stops being fragile when it’s baked into systems and rituals, not just resting on the personality of one good leader.

Recognizing Unsafe Leadership Environments
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/recognizing-unsafe-leadership-environments
Post 9 of the Unaccountable series examines how unsafe leadership environments reveal themselves through patterns that restrict correction, narrow truth, and reorganize systems around protecting authority.

Stop Turning Your Team Into Opponents
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/stop-turning-your-team-into-opponents
This post warns leaders against creating competition and scarcity inside their teams, reminding that true leadership builds trust, clarity, and shared success.

Teams Are Not Wells
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/teams-are-not-wells
A leadership reflection contrasting extractive management with investment-driven leadership, reminding that teams thrive when they’re nurtured, not drained.

The 7 Conditions: Alignment
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-alignment-when-the-direction-actually-holds
Alignment is not agreement in a meeting. It is whether the organization is actually moving in the same direction when leadership is not in the room. Most organizations have the appearance of alignment. Very few have the substance of it.

The 7 Conditions: Clarity
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-clarity-what-actually-lands
Clarity is not the certainty a leader feels. It is whether the people on their team actually know what matters, right now, in the work they are doing today. The gap between those two things is where organizations quietly lose.

The 7 Conditions: Communication
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-communication-the-message-that-actually-arrives
Communication is not whether the leader talks. It is whether the message arrives. The gap between what was sent and what was received is where organizations lose decisions, lose time, and lose people.

The 7 Conditions: Consistency
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-consistency-the-leader-people-learn-to-predict
Consistency is not about being the same person every day. It is about whether the people on your team can predict what you stand for, what you will enforce, and how you will show up when it costs you something.

The 7 Conditions: Drift
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-drift-the-condition-that-moves-without-permission
Drift is not a failure. It is the direction failure travels in when no one is watching closely enough. It is measurable, predictable, and almost always well underway before the people responsible for fixing it know it exists.

The 7 Conditions: Stability
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-stability-the-environment-that-holds
Stability is not the absence of pressure. It is whether the environment holds under it. A stable organization can absorb difficulty without lurching. An unstable one cannot, and the people inside it spend more energy bracing than building.

The 7 Conditions: Trust
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-trust-the-condition-that-cannot-be-declared
Trust is not a feeling. It is the conclusion a team draws from a pattern they have been watching for months. It cannot be declared, mandated, or assumed. It is built through behavior or it does not exist at all.

The 7 Conditions: Why This Series Exists
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-the-7-conditions-why-this-series-exists
We are going to spend the next seven posts talking about the conditions that determine whether your leadership is actually working. Not whether you think it is working. Whether the people living inside the system you have built experience it as working.

The Approval Economy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-approval-economy
When favoritism takes hold inside a team, something predictable happens. People stop optimizing for results and start optimizing for access. This entry examines how that shift happens, what it does to team culture, and why servant leadership demands a different economy entirely.

The Case for Servant Leadership (Part 1)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-case-for-servant-leadership-part-1
After a hard stop and deep reflection, the evidence and experience align: servant leadership is the only model that sustains both people and performance.

The Case for Servant Leadership (Part 2)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-case-for-servant-leadership-part-2
What the data actually says: neuroscience, engagement, and innovation studies confirm why servant leadership outperforms pressure-based models.

The Case for Servant Leadership (Part 3)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-case-for-servant-leadership-part-3
How fear sneaks into leadership, hijacks the brain, spreads through culture, then how service quiets fear and rebuilds trust.

The Case for Servant Leadership (Part 4)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-case-for-servant-leadership-part-4
The Logic of Leadership: five principles, Clarity, Protect the Culture, Build Trust Daily, Empower Over Control, Serve the Standard, validated by research and experience.

The Case for Servant Leadership (Part 5)
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-case-for-servant-leadership-part-5
Archetype Original: rebirth from realization, turning proven leadership logic into systems leaders can live out today.

The Cost of Calm
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-cost-of-calm
Calm under pressure isn’t always healthy. This post unpacks the personal cost of high-functioning leadership, offering five lessons on recovery, boundaries, and balance for leaders who carry too much weight.

The Cult of Confusion
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/cult-of-confusion
Narcissistic leaders use ambiguity, contradiction, and withheld information to maintain control. Servant leaders use clarity, consistency, and shared truth to build trust.

The Data Caught Up
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-data-caught-up
New research confirms what Holding the Line already argued: unenforced standards don't stay neutral, they quietly become the culture.

The Distraction Play
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-distraction-play
Narcissistic leaders keep teams busy with noise to avoid accountability. Servant leaders resist constant pivots and focus attention on what truly matters.

The Golden Rule Has Always Been a Leadership Strategy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/golden-rule-leadership-strategy
The Golden Rule isn’t sentimental, it’s structural. It reveals the health of a leader, exposes misused power, and shapes the kind of culture people can rise inside of.

The Illusion of Control
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/illusion-of-control
Narcissistic leaders maintain control through hierarchy, ambiguity, and blame, creating the illusion of strength while hiding incompetence. Servant leaders lead by clarity, empowerment, and shared responsibility.

The Invisible Tax
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-invisible-tax
Favoritism doesn't announce itself. It moves through an organization quietly, below the visible line of leadership behavior, and by the time the damage is measurable, it has already been paid for. This entry examines how favoritism forms, what it costs, and why the first condition for fixing it is being willing to look.

The Isolation Strategy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-isolation-strategy
Narcissistic leaders engineer distance to avoid accountability and truth. Servant leaders move toward their teams, building trust through presence, proximity, and transparency.

The Joseph Archetype
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-joseph-archetype
The Joseph Archetype is the story of a leader who absorbed betrayal, stayed faithful in obscurity, and arrived at power without bitterness in the hand that held it. The second entry in The Archetype Series.

The Judas Archetype
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-judas-archetype
The Judas Archetype is not a story about a monster. It is a story about a pattern. One that does not begin with betrayal. It ends there. And the path between the beginning and the end looks, for most of its length, like ordinary leadership behavior.

The Loyalty Economy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-loyalty-economy
Narcissistic leaders build in-groups, out-groups, and conditional loyalty. Servant leaders cultivate principled unity, honest input, and shared purpose.

The Manufactured Crisis
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/manufactured-crisis
Narcissistic leaders manufacture urgency to maintain control and relevance. Servant leaders calm the room, steward pressure wisely, and protect their teams from emotional volatility.

The Pendulum of Leadership: Efficiency vs. Empathy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-pendulum-of-leadership-efficiency-vs-empathy
This post explores the constant tension leaders face between efficiency and empathy, showing how real leadership lies in managing the balance between clarity and compassion.

The Performance of Perfection
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/performance-of-perfection
Narcissistic leaders hide behind curated competence and flawless optics. Servant leaders choose transparency, courage, and truth over image.

The Producer-Leader Paradox
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-producer-leader-paradox
When leaders are forced to keep producing at full speed, their ability to actually lead withers. The Producer-Leader Paradox exposes how short-term output kills long-term growth.

The Proxy Trap
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-proxy-trap
Narcissistic leaders rely on high performers to mask their weaknesses, using them as proxies for competence. Servant leaders build capacity, share credit, and grow alongside their teams.

The Pursuit of Power Corrupts
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-pursuit-of-power-corrupts
A reflection on how the pursuit of power can erode trust and culture, contrasting power-hungry leadership with servant-hearted leadership that carries people, not control.

The Saul Archetype
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-saul-archetype
Saul was chosen, transformed, and given every advantage a leader could ask for. His fall came from what he chose to protect once he had power, not from how he started. The third entry in The Archetype Series traces the decisions that took him from anointed to unrecognizable.

The Scarcity Switch
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-scarcity-switch
Narcissistic leaders weaponize scarcity to maintain control. Servant leaders create abundance through clarity, trust, and shared opportunity.

The Shadow of Shame
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/shadow-of-shame
When leaders deflect, distort, or redirect blame, shame becomes a silent toxin inside the culture. Servant leaders face truth instead of shielding themselves from it.

The System Behind the Conditions
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/ali-series-introducing-the-archetype-leadership-index
Seven conditions. Eight posts. One question that has been building from the beginning. If these conditions matter, and the evidence says they do, how do you actually measure them honestly from both sides of the leadership relationship?

The Work Is Now Public
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/the-work-is-now-public
A reflection on the public launch of Archetype Original and the decision to make decades of leadership work visible and accessible.

Toxic Empathy
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/toxic-empathy
An examination of toxic empathy in leadership, how avoidance disguises itself as care, and why servant leadership requires the strength to hold both presence and accountability.

Toxic Empathy Post 2: Holding the Line
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/toxic-empathy-2-holding-the-line
A close examination of the exact moment leaders hesitate, why the decision to hold the line is difficult, and how avoiding that cost reshapes standards, effort, and culture in real time.

Toxic Empathy Post 3: The Cost of Avoidance
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/toxic-empathy-3-the-cost-of-avoidance
An examination of what actually happens when leaders avoid necessary action, how behavior is shaped through response, and how unaddressed issues compound into system-wide consequences over time.

Toxic Empathy Post 4: Servant Leadership Role
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/toxic-empathy-4-servant-leadership-role
A correction of servant leadership as a disciplined practice, clarifying the difference between empathy that serves and empathy that avoids responsibility, and how leaders must hold both awareness and accountability at the same time.

Toxic Empathy Post 5: The Conversation
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/toxic-empathy-5-the-conversation
A practical look at what it actually means to lead through difficult conversations, how clarity is established in real time, and why holding both empathy and responsibility is what moves people and systems forward.

We’ve Lost the Plot on Leadership.
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/weve-lost-the-plot-on-leadership
A call to return to servant leadership, rejecting the pursuit of power and ego in favor of service, sacrifice, and the courage to lead through humility.

What are your values as a business leader?
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/what-are-your-values-as-a-business-leader
A challenge to leaders to live their values instead of performing them, reminding that real integrity is proven through behavior, not branding.

When Leaders Refuse to Change: The Enron Lesson
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/when-leaders-refuse-to-change-the-enron-lesson
A deep leadership case study drawn from Enron’s collapse, exploring how ego, fear, and denial destroy organizations, and why humility and adaptability are essential for survival.

When Leadership Sank Kodak
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/when-leadership-sank-kodak
Kodak's downfall wasn't technological—it was a failure of leadership. A reflection on fear, control, and how servant leadership could have saved an empire built on memories.

When Responsibility Stops Being Accidental
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/when-responsibility-stops-being-accidental
A research-informed editorial on what happens when leaders refuse accountability, how pressure gets redirected downward, harm becomes intentional, and teams absorb consequences leaders won’t own.

When Stability Replaces Accountability
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/when-stability-replaces-accountability
An editorial examination of why leadership systems often protect stability at the expense of accountability, and why servant leadership cannot survive in environments that avoid responsibility.

Where We Begin
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/where-we-begin
Why Archetype Original begins with a 365-day Servant Leadership Devotional grounded in the Golden Rule.

Why I Chose the Name Archetype Original
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/why-i-chose-the-name-archetype-original
A foundational explanation of the name Archetype Original, what it means, why it was chosen, and the conviction about leadership, alignment, and responsibility that sits beneath the work.

Why Leaders Hire Me
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/why-leaders-hire-me
Why experienced leaders choose to work with me to sharpen judgment, reduce unforced errors, and grow without costing their people.

Why Servant Leadership Disrupts Unaccountable Power
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/why-servant-leadership-disrupts-unaccountable-power
Post 7 of the Unaccountable series marks the pivot from diagnosis to disruption, examining how Servant Leadership restores consequence to insulated systems and exposes authority that cannot survive accountability.

You Don’t Win Alone. Ever.
https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/journal/you-dont-win-alone-ever
A first-person editorial on why leadership is never a solo act, and how retreating under pressure often accelerates failure instead of preventing it.


Devotional series

The Servant Leadership Devotional Series publishes one entry per day, connecting Scripture to the real pressures of leadership. Formation for leaders who want clarity, restraint, and genuine care for people.

Published devotional entries: 205.
Browse the series at https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/faith.
Individual devotional URLs are listed in https://www.archetypeoriginal.com/sitemap.xml.



Generated at: 2026-07-24T16:52:25.617Z
